displayTitle: 2026-06-18 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Transport
description: Thursday, 18 June 2026 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Transport Hearing of Evidence: Minister for Rail Rt Hon Winston Peters Suzanne Tindall, Chairperson (KiwiRail Holdings Limited) Peter Reidy, Chief Executive (KiwiRail Holdings Limited) Chris Mackenzie, Chairperson (Ferry Holdings) Sandip Ranchod, Chief Executive (Ferry Holdings) Minister of Transport Hon Chris Bishop Brett Gliddon, Chief Executive Reuben Levermore, Chief of Staff Mike Hargreaves, Director of Land Transport and Group General Manager – Regulatory Associate Minister of Transport Hon James Meager Brad Ward, Secretary for Transport (Acting) and Chief Executive Ruth Fairhall, Deputy Chief Executive – Policy Group David Wood, Deputy Chief Executive – Investment and Monitoring Group Paul O'Connell, Sector Strategy Group Carmen Mak, Deputy Chief Executive – Corporate Services Chris Nees, Chief of Staff (Acting) David Kidman, Chief Financial Officer (Acting) Ministry of Transport—Te Manatū Waka Brad Ward, Chief Executive (Acting) Ruth Fairhall, Deputy Chief Executive – Policy Chris Nees, Chief of Staff (Acting) David Wood, Deputy Chief Executive – Investment and Monitoring Paul O'Connell, Deputy Chief Executive – Sector Strategy Carmen Mak, Deputy Chief Executive – Corporate Services David Kidman, Chief Financial Officer New Zealand Transport Agency—Waka Kotahi Brett Gliddon, Chief Executive Cassandra Crowley, Deputy Chairperson Karen Jones, Group General Manager – Engagement and Partnership Kevin Doherty, Group General Manager – Transport Services Mike Hargreaves, Director of Land Transport and Group General Manager – Regulatory Services Richard Forgan, Group General Manager – System Leadership Sara Lindsay, Group General Manager – Commercial and Corporate Sarina Pratley, Chief Customer and Services Officer
descriptionSummary: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Transport. Minister for Rail Rt Hon Winston Peters. Suzanne Tindall, Chairperson (KiwiRail Holdings Limited)

chunks.text: We can put this, yeah, absolutely.

This way. No answer. I just- One more minute. So It's, he does come right on the minute he just said. You really don't want to? Well, it's not our problem. No, it's Simon's. Right. I'll just pause it. Should be here. Right. Well, still got a minute. Still got a minute.

Probably right on the minute. He can't- Get in. Keep going. You want a pass? No, it's all right. Oh, there you go. You saw him. Sir? He's coming. He's just coming. Yep. He's on time. There he is. See. Just on time Phone calls flying for get you here.

Right. Okay. We can now start. So welcome, Minister, and welcome to all the officials as well. Good to have you here. We usually open with some comments, but I know you've already given us some comments in writing, so say what you wanted to say, and otherwise we'll go straight to questions.

All right. Good morning, and thank you for the chance to deliver the goods for Rail once again. We are joined by Kiwi Rail chair, Suzanne Tindall, Ferry Holdings chair, Chris McKenzie, and their respective chief executives, Peter Reedy and Sandip Ranchod. The rail portfolio has come into stark relief in recent months with the spike in oil prices, just as it did in the 1970s.

Rail freight and passenger rail proves its worth when there is a price crunch. In financial terms, Kiwi Rail is on track to deliver its 160 million earnings target this financial year by June 30, 2026. That is up from last year when it exceeded its 110 million earnings target.

Interislander reliability is at 98%, Air NZ must look on with envy of that result. Of note, in 2021, it was 81% reliable, then 83% reliable in 2022, and has picked up steadily since then. This is due to a substantial increase in maintenance investment and great work by crews and management. Likewise, rail freight service reliability is up too. That matters because turning up on time is what matters in freight. That is why rail freight volumes are up 7% this year versus road freight increasing by 2%. You're witnessing market share shifting to rail.

We want to put on record our thanks to Ms. Tindall, her board, and the whole team at Kiwi Rail because we are seeing a firm, disciplined focus on performance. Railway workers will look back on this period in their history as the turning point for long-term success. Regarding the ferries, our thanks are also given to Mr.

McKenzie, his board, and the whole team at Ferry Holdings. In July, we will announce the details of a long-term commercial set of arrangements, sensible, affordable port fees. The ferry operator, the secure future for freighters and families on the Cook Strait, all for 2.3 billion less than the ridiculous IRX program. This is a project that all parliamentarians should learn from. Some in this committee have designs on the transport portfolio in their futures.

You would do well to study what it looks like to drive performance based on scope control, to drop the magpie attraction to shiny designs, and appoint competent men and women to deliver only what is needed to do the job. Take city rail link, for example. When the government in 2021, when IRX was approved, tried to do everything, everywhere, all at once with many different infrastructure projects. It placed huge demand on the small construction sector, and prices then shot up. The blowout there was an inside job.

Instead, we need to have an agreed infrastructure pipeline, spend more than 60 cents on every dollar on maintenance and renewal, and sequence major projects in a steady manner that drives supply through stable demand. That is what we are doing in rail already. Some politicians have ambitions for sainthood when they just need to attend confession.

The good news is we are providing a lesson in good management with rail. We welcome any questions. Thank you. Thank you. Right. First question, Julie Anne Genter, then Kerry. Is design work still being undertaken on the ferry project, or is that concluded?

Which ferry is she talking about? The Interislander ferries that you just referred to. New ferries. Well, the new ferries, the new ferry design work is continuing. Yeah. So if the design work's still underway and there's not a fixed price or capped contract, how can the government be confident about the delivery price and timing?

Or is there a fixed price? We have a fixed price contract with the shipyard. With the shipyard, but what about the rest of the work? So- You're talking about the wharves and- Yeah... and so forth? Landside. We are going through contracts at the moment. Okay. And we have identified areas where we will provide the product that is required to berth the ferries and the equipment that goes with it, all within the 1.7.

So there won't be any costs that needs to be met by the regional council or the port owners? Yes. No. The regional council will not be up for anything more. Yeah, but there are some costs that they're meeting- Yes... that will be laid out there. Yeah.

Okay. So in- Every question you're asking here has been put out in press statements and answered to you before. Sure. The only one there is the July announcement. Well, I guess I'm just trying to- There's only fact that's new... be assured that the full cost of the project is comparable to what was happening before in terms of what the government share is versus overall costs.

No, because excuse me, I don't want anything like what was happening before. What was happening before had blowouts so that the price of the ferries was actually, in the end, could have been less than 20% of the total cost. Yeah. Over 80% would've been on infrastructure that no one knew about. Right. So- I hope we're not going to do that. I'm just wondering if there's still just- By the way, have you studied Tasmania and see what they did?

They did exactly what the IRX program did. They got the two ferries all right. No infrastructure. Now they can't even use the ferries. That's where we were heading, and I'm never going to back off on that So there's a fixed price for the vessels, for the ships- Yes... but not for the work on the wharves? Contracts haven't been let yet.

So we don't know the total cost of the project yet? The board has made it very clear to Sandip and his team- Yeah... we are not going a dollar over 1.7 billion. And so we will look at the ways that we can achieve that. We've achieved some, for example, in Picton, and I told you last time we were here, where originally we were going to transition Bluebridge and Interislander across.

We don't have to do that anymore. We had a smart infrastructure person who came in and described a way that we could do it. It saves us time, and it saves us money. And we are continuing to do that. In November 2025, ministers announced that suggested user charges would be sufficient to repay Crown funding and provide for future ferry replacement.
Is that still the Crown's position?

Say that again. Say that again. The minister's announcements in November 2025 suggested that user charges would be sufficient to repay Crown funding and provide for ferry replacement in the future. Well, if there was a change, we'd have announced it. We haven't changed our announcement back in November '25.

That's the position still. Okay. That's how you run government. You keep your word, unless circumstances dramatically change where you have to point out the difference. We have not made that difference or seen that difference- Just one more final- Last, last one then we've got to get to Sup next. Yeah, yeah, yeah. So how has- No, I'm trying to get, I'm trying to get to the point of your question. Yeah An announcement is made. If the circumstances had changed, we'd announce the change.

Has the conflict in Iran and higher fuel prices affected project costings? Not at this point. No. Not at this point. Okay. Dave Sup. Thank you, Mr. Chair. My question is for Peter, CEO of Kiwi Rail. The minister said last year that the cost of the ferries will be offset by the Interislander paying the port fees.

Just wondering if that's still the case, and if so, how Kiwi Rail is able to fund that, and if not, how will Kiwi Rail fund that? We're still in negotiation with Ferry Holdings on the overall commercial agreements. And we are collaborating extremely well with Ferry Holdings and the ports.

So the whole package of how this looks is still work in progress. Okay, so no indication of likely how it will affect Kiwi Rail's balance sheet as yet? Well, the work we're doing is we understand our demand model going forward. And we've made that, and we've shared that with Sandip and the team.

So we're very aligned around the demand, we're very aligned around the affordability of what we need to run the operation, and as the operator that's our job. And Ferry Holdings are doing a great job at taking that information and working with all of us on what would fees be, for example, for terminal fees.

Okay. Thank you. Thanks. Thank you. Yes, thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you, Minister and team. Can I just go back to the fact that final contracts haven't necessarily been let for the full project? What protections are in place to manage delay in terms of delivery, contract failure, cost overruns, those sorts of things?

So I hear that you say the board has been very clear, not a dollar more than this figure. But what is actually going to be put in place to mitigate cost overruns, failure to deliver? Well, one could give a thousand answers from this position right here, but the main answer is we're not putting up with cost plus anymore.

We've gone through four decades of cost plus. Total rip-offs like Transmission Gully, CityRail link in Auckland, $2 billion overspend. I mean, we're not putting up with any of that anymore. And out there, it's clear in the market that the government's view on this level of future performance has changed dramatically.

We expect them to do contracts and be completed on time and within budget. And the last time that happened was the second Tauranga Harbor Bridge. So- And guess who organized that? So what is specifically being put as part of the contractual negotiations to ensure that, as the minister has said, that that is not going to happen?

It's all good and well saying, "The board's made it very clear, not a dollar more." But what is actually being done to ensure that actually that is the outcome? From a board point of view, we have made it very clear to Sandip and his crew in negotiations we will not be going over our budget. Sandip's probably better to answer because he's in the middle of doing the negotiations and so forth at the moment.

Yeah, sure. Designs have been developed. They've been priced by builders who we have at the table now on each port. Those prices have been submitted to us. They find their way into a contract, and target prices get used with an incentive pain-gain regime to manage the cost. On top of that, we work collaboratively with the ports to monitor construction.

Those rules of racing have been written up. The builders understand them, and we get started on those when they hit the ground, which has started now. So part of the issue as to why the negotiations for the existing closure took so long after Nicola Willis decided to cancel the project without any alternative in place was the need for a cancellation component. Are there cancellation components being entertained as part of these contractual negotiations?

So we're using standard form contracts, and they have those sorts of exit provisions in them. Okay. Can I just say, we have also announced that we've got hundreds of millions in contingency factored into our planning on this matter. And the other thing that's most critical is cost plus is not really going to be written into the contract.

No. That's the fundamental difference. It's not going to be in the contract. Minister, what assurances can you give that the new ferries will be delivered and in service by the timeframe that you've set of 2029? And who ultimately will be held accountable if that timeframe is not met?

Well, the- You? Yes, I will be. I probably will still be here to oversee that they get it done properly. But the Chinese have given me to know that, believe without it becoming a contractual matter, that they're out to deliver earlier than they've committed themselves to.

Having been there to looked at their operation and how big it is and how enthusiastic they were for potential future contracts with New Zealand, that they'll go gangbusters to do what they said they can do. So are you then saying that the full package for the ferries could potentially be delivered earlier?

Yes. Port infrastructure, everything. Well, that's the other thing. I mean, why not? The reality is, if I can just give you an example. There was a fire at Wellington Airport, right? The planning to fix this looks like it's going to be months away. I was looking at it the other day. There's no 24/7 working.

This is your capital city airport, and look at the laxity and approach they take. That's got to be expunged from New Zealand's business from here on in, government or private business. We've got to get back on track as a country. We're going to have to start working our a*s and doing the job and fulfilling contracts in the way that other first world countries do. We used to do it when we had nobody but a horse and cart in the good old days under Burgoyne, when he built more railways in 10 years than the next 130 years. So it's a matter of bad attitude and governance, really.

In terms of the fact that... Just one more. One more. Thank you. Currently, there is no rail-enabled capacity out of the existing fleet at the moment that's operating the strait. So in terms of risk to national supply chain opportunities through rail, what has actually been put in place to mitigate some of that as a concern?

It might be for Mr. Reid perhaps. Sure. I'll take that. So firstly, we were in consultation with the market in December last year, looking at the options and would the market pay for another ship. The market, and when I say our market, I mean our customers, made it quite clear, no, they wouldn't be prepared to do that, but they wanted to look at options should a ferry go down, or if Bluebridge's ferry went down as well.

So we went to the market, and from a business continuity perspective, we've put in place if a ferry goes down while the Kaitaki is in dry dock for three months. So this is during sort of July, August, September. If a ferry goes down, then we've got a coastal solution, which is slower freight, but the customers have said, "We would allocate the right sort of freight to go on coastal."

We've been to the market. They accept that. That's happened in previous times when our critical infrastructure has been out, like the Kokura earthquake. Now, if, for example, there was a catastrophic disaster where a ferry was out for several months, then we'd obviously look at other options as well. And we've done this in the past when we bought the Valentine out. But at the moment, contingency is for a coastal feed, and we've already got rates in place, and we've already communicated those to the market.

Is there a concern- Hang on, so that's the last one. Sorry. Yeah. Actually, I'll have a question for you later. Okay. I'm going to take a quick sup on that one if I can, because we're doing the ports inquiry, and the issue of coastal shipping has actually come up quite a lot. The relationship you have there that you're talking about as a contingency is with the coastal shippers, or is it with the international shippers?

It's SWAT. SWAT. Okay. So that's important for us to know. How important is that to you? Oh, critical. I mean, it's part of the transport system. Okay. That's helpful. Thank you. Dan. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Question for Chris, just in relation to the infrastructure for Wellington and for Picton. This government's got a big focus on value for money, so I'm just wondering if your organization is focused on using cost benefit analysis or more specifically, the Treasury better business case methodology in developing the port infrastructure,

and in deciding the exact infrastructure requirements for those two ports. Again, Sandra will give you the detail, but from a board perspective, we started out with what is the ship dimensions. What is then required at the two ports to enable our ships to berth.

That meant in Picton basically destroying, if you like, good Ministry of Works structures that were still very good from 1961. And if we hadn't changed the dimensions of the ship, we could have used those pieces of infrastructure still. Undersolds and so forth, we have to go from 19 to 28.5 meters.

So we couldn't use it. In Wellington, we had a maximum reuse, and the maximum reuse has been put in place for everything apart from the link spans because the new vessels are a different dimension So in some ways, we started at a disadvantage in that we had a project which has just dropped on us, two ships and two ports.

Mm-hmm. So we didn't do a lot of that planning and business case work. And so through Sandip and his team, we have identified what are the options that we have, and we have been working with Treasury. Yep. And the options that we have come up with that Sandip was talking about going through with contractors, that has delivered for us a set of plans which now in the contract negotiations, both with KiwiRail as the current operator and the two ports, is going to deliver us what is required- This one... at the minimum cost.

Yeah. Because that's one of the things that we're required for, minimum cost and on time for the end of 2028. Mm-hmm. So business cases and so forth sort of missed out because of the project, the way it started, and our delivery time. Great. Thank you.

I'm going to take two more sups on this. You had a new area, didn't you, Julie Ann? Yeah, I have. So I'm going to take a sup and a sup, and then I'm going to have a primary to Julie Ann. Because you just now- There we go. Thank you, Minister. Just now you mentioned about you're negotiating with the contractor for this construction project or the rebuild for the port.

But as my understanding, there's a few project only got one bidder to actually bid for the project. Do you think it's a concern? Do you think because without any competition, the cost of doing the project will be higher? Is that a concern for you? Yeah. Sandip. Yeah. So there were processes to select the bidders for the construction.

They were run by our delivery partners, who are the ports, and we sat next to them through that process. In Picton, there was a competitive process that selected a contractor to enter into a pricing phase, and Hy- Hyvinchi won that. And in Bryan Perry being selected by Wellington, that went through a process as well. They're known to those companies.

The additional measure that we've put on to mitigate the risk that you're talking about- Mm... is reconciliation of price to prove value for money. So we have independent estimates look over those construction estimates and have to reconcile those estimates as the design packages come through. Thank you.

Can I just check the ownership? And I don't think the ownership's changed of the port infrastructure from what was proposed before to now. The Port of Marlborough was and is owned by Port of Marlborough, the improvements there. In Wellington, KiwiRail owns the, or the Interisland Line owns that bit of the port, from my recollection.

And so is that infrastructure going to be owned by KiwiRail/Interisland Line, or is it going to be owned by Centreport? As far as infrastructure is concerned, leaving the ships aside, the Crown will own the link spans and the associated maritime pieces that go with that.

At both ends or just the Wellington end? At both ends. At both ends. Okay, Bryan. Marlborough District Council, through their holdings, owns Port in Picton. Yep. And they have recently agreed to contribute 110. Yep. What that has meant in Picton, and it's been made public previously, is that there will be a vehicle put together which will be a mix of Crown and Port, and they will own the infrastructure as a proportion. So in Picton, it's roughly 70 to 30, but the board that runs that special purpose vehicle will be

50/50. The port operates the port, but the Crown will always retain ownership of the assets that we have paid for. 70 or 30? At Centreport, it's slightly different in that we're not building a new wharf. Yeah. So the Crown and Wellington will own the link span and associated works that go with it.

Land is a different issue because land is owned by the port over in Picton. In Wellington, there's a mixture of KiwiRail and port land that they work across. No, that's helpful. And I wanted to go to a completely different area, and that is the opportunities for the rail network for new links which you've been working on.

Obviously, we know about the Marsden link, but I know that there are others which KiwiRail have significant aspirations for. So I'm just interested in what you are seeing as the opportunities to expand the network or to expand services, whether that is freight or passenger, across the network.

To Cameron. Sure. I'll take that. So firstly, from a metro perspective, you've obviously, post CRL- Yep... there's an opportunity there to expand more capacity through that line. The idea would be to have a separation of freight and metro, like you do in large modern cities. And as the patronage forecasts are getting up to 50 million passengers per annum, then that will be a requirement.

So firstly, a fourth main is critical. So as you come up to Papakura, should go left and right and separate that out. That would allow freight train access in and out of Auckland, and there's 50 trains a day right now in and out of Auckland in terms of freight. So the national freight supply market starts at Auckland and it ends in Auckland.

And you have to leave at a certain time from Auckland to connect with the Cook Strait ferries. So firstly, on a freight perspective, it's the Golden Triangle. If we could electrify the Golden Triangle and there's passing loops there, so 50% of the freight growth going forward, in particular commodities, is going to be in the Golden Triangle. So there's some capacity opportunities there.

S- Thirdly, in Metro there's a large opportunity from an urban development perspective to look at the east of Auckland around Mount Roskill, and that involves the Avondale Southdown corridor, which if that was opened up, that is an urban development Metro opportunity, and it obviously benefits freight as well.

The other opportunity is selected freight hubs, intermodal freight hubs. We're looking at one now in Mosgiel, and that allows consolidation of freight into a central area. So there's selectively key parts of our freight network in terms of putting in passing loops to get more capacity. We're working on right now the investment we're getting from the government that's going to help us speed the trains up in terms of reducing speed restrictions.

So getting more out of our existing network as opposed to building a lot of new rail networks. But the big growth, particularly in Auckland, would be thinking about how we can separate the metro and freight. Okay. So Avondale Southdown is critical- Yes... for that. Yes. And so the council and the discussions with the council, I understand there have been some discussions with the council, how we try to encourage them through focus intensification in that area to help pay for the infrastructure as opposed to going somewhere else.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. There have been some initial discussions at official level. So Peter and I, with the CEO of Auckland Council, Minister Peters has met with Mayor Brown. They are encouraging us to work together. To answer your question, and obviously disclosure, I was the CFO of Auckland Council- So I understand... some years ago.

So this is very much in my wheelhouse. The Avondale Southdown urban development case would unlock approximately 13,000 new homes. That is 13,000 new ratable units for Auckland Council as well, as well as other commercial activity around that corridor. The important piece also is that designation is in place until 2030. So we are, Peter and I, again working with the officials at Auckland Council to give effect to that designation.

Excellent. No, thank you. Julian. Will the government be funding an upgrade to the signaling system in Wellington? We had a train derail because there's long overdue, the upgrade was needed years ago, but it needs government funding, and clearly passenger rail in Wellington is a really important part of our network. We have high levels of patronage.

Look, I'll let my colleagues answer that question, but contemplating $750 million to make up for human error is not, in my view, a smart economic strategy, nor is it a smart governmental or business strategy either. What we need to do is ensure that the people who are doing the job do their job properly. So we train them to the max and make sure that they're alert and making sure that they're capable of doing what they're expected to. Because even in the upgrade in Auckland, still saw what you might call four examples of the red lights being run

in recent times. So are you saying that you don't think we need to upgrade the signaling system? I thought it was like over $1 billion you're talking about. I'm just ask-- like, do you think that we don't-- we-- that's a nice to have, that we don't need to upgrade our signaling system to be consistent with other passenger rail networks? If we had the money, we'd do a whole lot of other things as well.

But the question is, is it vitally necessary to get the performance that one wants? So how many train derailments do you think we should accept? Well, it wasn't actually a train derailment, was it? That's the first thing that's wrong about that statement. Can we just clarify? I can probably pick that up. So officially, there has been over the last three years, it's been back in '24, 110 million, '25, 144 million, last year, this year's budget, 106 million to the two metros.

You've seen significant improvement in the Auckland Metro. Wellington, there's lots of work being done on the Wairarapa network, so investment is going in. Just on the Johnson-- well, the Kendalla incident, so MetLink and Transdev have publicly said that that train passed a red signal. That's the first thing.

It's under investigation, so I won't go into any other further discussion on that. The second thing is the key point is the signal system worked safely. It failed safely. So the signaling system in Wellington is safe. Hmm. Whenever you go-- if you go through a red light, and we don't operate the trains in Wellington, but when the operators do, all the signals go to red, so it fails safely. The particular incident in Kendalla, it failed safely. And not only that, the signaling system diverted the train onto a separate rail

spur that we had designed through government funding a year ago. So, we are continually investing the money in signaling systems both in Wellington and Auckland. In fact, in Auckland right now, there's a lot of work going on in the southern area to post CRL. So it's a big part of the investment of we get from government in the, is in the signaling area.

Thank you. We are at time, but really appreciate it. Thank you for all the answers to questions. If there's any final thing you wanted to say, otherwise, we'll call it there. Well, government can be boring, but actually dealing with railways, which is got-- it's been a rather Cinderella rise from marginalized- Yes... parts of our history. It's actually quite exciting.

So I advise any of you, if you have aspirations for this job, one of the best portfolios you can have. You're actually doing something progressive that'll be around 50, 60, 100 years from now Indeed. Thank you all very much, really appreciate it. Thank you. Thanks. That's good. Sweating here. Thanks, Stephen. Sorry. It's a missile We better get the next minister.

Long story short. It was a conference last year. Oh, really? It was budgeted, travel was budgeted at $30,000, it cost $60,000. There was a mistake with they budgeted economy flights. It was a 20 hours flight, so they upgraded to business class, and then under budgeted land transport.
So long story short, it was a budgetary error in assistance, and everything else was within the guidelines. Great. I'm going. I wouldn't even submit the question.
Sorry. Simon. Simon. You got in. You cut it fine. Hurry. Right. No worries.

Welcome. Hello again. Nice to see you. Long time no see. Good to be here. Yeah. Right. So I think what we've got, we've got you here for the first 45, and then James for the second 45. Is that the way we're going to do it or we- No, I think- Do you want to tag team?

Yeah. How do you want to do it? Well, you've only got me for half. Sorry? You've only got me for half an hour. Oh, God. James Meagher for half an hour is all, not for a full- Right. Okay. For half an hour at the... Okay. Cool. Good. That's fine. But that's the way we're doing it, one after the other. Okay, cool. Right. Okay. In the usual form, great to have you here. If you want to make any opening statements, we always look forward to that.

And you know there's going to be plenty of questions, so we look forward to ripping into that. I can't wait. Well, thanks for having me. Good to see you all again. I've got Honorable James Meagher as my associate here. He deals with range of delegated matters. Obviously, he's got Brad Ward here, chief executive of, or acting executive of Ministry of Transport prior to MCERT. Brett Flynn here from the Transport Agency, MR- Actually, do you want to come up to the table, Brett, as well?

And anybody else who want to- It's getting ahead of like, but okay. Then a range of senior officials here from across the agencies. Busy times in transport, is a massive work program, as you can see, both at a policy level led by MoT and at a delivery level through NZTA, and other agencies as well. Any number of different things we could talk about, but probably just a couple to highlight.

One is the move towards RUC reform, move towards electronic road user charges, and a transition that frankly has to happen, as the National Transport Fund revenue becomes increasingly unsustainable because of electric vehicles and fuel efficient cars.

Which is a good thing, but does put a revenue pressure on the system. So we obviously got work underway around that. The Land Transport Revenue Bill, which has just come back to the House, thank you for the committee's hard work on that, is the sort of first step in that transition. We're not rushing it. It's important to get it right, build social license.

I would hope we could do it in a collegial and relatively bipartisan way. It will be one of the biggest reforms to the transport funding system, probably since in a generation, or possibly even two, depending on how you measure it. So really important we get that right. A number of other things that pop to mind.

Obviously, you've seen the National Infrastructure Plan response, and government's committed to a review of land transport funding, which is a separate issue to the, separate but connected issue to RUC and eRUC, and we're continuing to get on with that. But as I said to the committee a couple of days ago, we haven't decided on the scope or exactly how that will work, but we've just committed to it as part of the infrastructure plan response. Anyway, conscious I don't want to talk forever, and you'll have questions, so happy to just get into it.

Start off with Tangi. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you, ministers. When can we expect the draft GPS for land transport to come out? Well, that's a good question. We don't have a fixed date for it. This is for the 2027 period onwards, 1 July.

It is challenging because obviously we've been dealing with the fuel situation in the last few weeks and months, and that has discombobulated things somewhat. So there's a challenge there. And the most... I mean, the obvious issue that I think the committee is well aware of is the scheduled rise in FED and RUC, fuel excise duty and road user charges scheduled on the 1st of January 2027, which have to be legislated, and of course, haven't been legislated yet, and we've signaled are unlikely to go ahead.

So that factors into it as well. So I don't have a fixed date. Once we... We don't actually have to issue one. There's no pressing time pressure on it, put it that way. Right. But, I mean, well, there's no pressing time pressure, but obviously we need one both consulted and finalized, in advance of 1 July '27.
